source_paper_title: Weak Approachability of Convex Sets in Absorbing Games

context:
Page 4, Introduction (end of main contributions summary)

Weak determinacy for Big-Match games of type II remains a problem.
title: Establish weak determinacy for convex target sets in Big-Match type II
background_latex:
A (two-player, zero-sum) generalized quitting game has finite action sets split into non-quitting and quitting actions: player 1 has actions \(I=I^{\mathrm{nq}}\cup I^{\star}\) and player 2 has actions \(J=J^{\mathrm{nq}}\cup J^{\star}\). A stage payoff vector \(g(i,j)\in\mathbb{R}^d\) is associated to each action pair. The game repeats over stages \(t=1,2,\dots\); once any quitting action is played for the first time (i.e., some \(i\in I^{\star}\) or \(j\in J^{\star}\)), the game absorbs and from then on the same action pair (hence the same payoff vector) repeats forever.

A Big-Match game of type II is the special case where player 2 has quitting actions but player 1 does not:
1) \(I^{\star}=\varnothing\) and \(J^{\star}\neq\varnothing\),
2) \(J^{\mathrm{nq}}\neq\varnothing\) (so player 2 also has at least one non-quitting action).
Thus player 1 always plays in \(I=I^{\mathrm{nq}}\) and player 2 plays in \(J=J^{\mathrm{nq}}\cup J^{\star}\).

A (behavioral) strategy for player 1 is a mapping \(\sigma\) from finite histories of non-absorbing action pairs \((I\times J^{\mathrm{nq}})^{t-1}\) to mixed actions \(\Delta(I)\). A strategy for player 2 is a mapping \(\tau\) from histories to \(\Delta(J^{\mathrm{nq}}\cup J^{\star})\).

Fix a closed convex target set \(C\subseteq\mathbb{R}^d\). For horizon \(T\in\mathbb{N}\), define the (Ces\`aro) time-average payoff
\[\bar g_T := \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^T g(i_t,j_t).\]
Let \(d_C(z):=\inf_{c\in C}\|z-c\|\) denote Euclidean distance to \(C\).

Weak approachability (in expectation) of \(C\) by player 1 means:
\[\forall\varepsilon>0,\ \exists T_\varepsilon\in\mathbb{N},\ \forall T\ge T_\varepsilon,\ \exists \sigma_T,\ \forall \tau,\ d_C\big(\mathbb{E}_{\sigma_T,\tau}[\bar g_T]\big)\le \varepsilon.\]
Weak excludability of \(C\) by player 2 means: there exists \(\delta>0\) such that player 2 can weakly approach the complement of the \(\delta\)-neighborhood of \(C\), i.e. ensure the expected average payoff stays at distance at least \(\delta\) from \(C\) for large known horizons.

A game is weakly determined for convex target sets if for every closed convex \(C\subseteq\mathbb{R}^d\), either \(C\) is weakly approachable by player 1 or \(C\) is weakly excludable by player 2.
problem_definition_latex:
Prove (or disprove) weak determinacy for Big-Match games of type II: for every Big-Match type II game with vector payoff \(g\) and every closed convex target set \(C\subseteq\mathbb{R}^d\), show that exactly one of the following holds:

1) \(C\) is weakly approachable by player 1, i.e.
\[\forall\varepsilon>0,\ \exists T_\varepsilon\in\mathbb{N},\ \forall T\ge T_\varepsilon,\ \exists \sigma_T,\ \forall \tau,\ d_C\big(\mathbb{E}_{\sigma_T,\tau}[\bar g_T]\big)\le \varepsilon;\]

or

2) \(C\) is weakly excludable by player 2, i.e. \(\exists\delta>0\) such that
\[\forall\varepsilon>0,\ \exists T_\varepsilon\in\mathbb{N},\ \forall T\ge T_\varepsilon,\ \exists \tau_T,\ \forall \sigma,\ d_C\big(\mathbb{E}_{\sigma,\tau_T}[\bar g_T]\big)\ge \delta-\varepsilon.\]

Equivalently, determine whether there exist Big-Match type II games and convex sets \(C\) that are neither weakly approachable nor weakly excludable, or prove that no such examples exist.

1) \(C\) is weakly approachable by player 1, i.e.
\[\forall\varepsilon>0,\ \exists T_\varepsilon\in\mathbb{N},\ \forall T\ge T_\varepsilon,\ \exists \sigma_T,\ \forall \tau,\ d_C\big(\mathbb{E}_{\sigma_T,\tau}[\bar g_T]\big)\le \varepsilon;\]

or

2) \(C\) is weakly excludable by player 2, i.e. \(\exists\delta>0\) such that
\[\forall\varepsilon>0,\ \exists T_\varepsilon\in\mathbb{N},\ \forall T\ge T_\varepsilon,\ \exists \tau_T,\ \forall \sigma,\ d_C\big(\mathbb{E}_{\sigma,\tau_T}[\bar g_T]\big)\ge \delta-\varepsilon.\]

location_in_paper: Page 4, Introduction (end of main contributions summary)
open_problem_quote: Weak determinacy for Big-Match games of type II remains a problem.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The game class is finite, so candidate counterexamples can be searched over small action sets and low-dimensional payoffs, but verifying weak approachability/excludability is itself nontrivial and may require solving auxiliary limiting or differential-game characterizations rather than a simple finite certificate.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can parameterize small Big-Match type II games and convex targets and use numerical proxies (e.g., finite-horizon minimax with horizon-dependent strategies, discretized differential-game value approximations) as a fitness signal to hunt for candidate indeterminate instances or supporting evidence for determinacy, but the signal may be expensive and only approximate the asymptotic weak notion.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper of Flesch–Laraki–Perchet extends Blackwell approachability to generalized quitting (absorbing) games and isolates three nested geometric conditions (1)–(3) built from the absorbing-game expression \(\frac{g+g^\star}{1+p^\star}\) inspired by Laraki’s explicit asymptotic-value formulas. They prove a one-sided characterization: Condition (1) is sufficient for weak approachability (via calibrated strategies), while failure of Condition (3) implies weak excludability. In Big-Match type I these conditions collapse to Blackwell’s classical condition, yielding weak determinacy; in Big-Match type II, however, the conditions separate and do not provide a full characterization.

For Big-Match type II, the paper gives a clean characterization of uniform approachability (Condition (1) \(\Leftrightarrow\) a Blackwell condition with constraints ensuring all quitting payoffs lie in \(C\)), but also constructs examples showing uniform determinacy fails and that Conditions (1)/(2) are not necessary and Condition (3) is not sufficient for weak approachability. They outline a continuous-time/differential-game approach in special cases (notably when player 2 has a single non-quitting action) reminiscent of Vieille’s differential-game method for weak approachability in classical repeated games, but they do not establish a general value/determinacy theorem in the absorbing setting.

As of the information provided here (and consistent with the paper’s conclusion), weak determinacy for convex target sets in Big-Match games of type II remains open: no general theorem proves that every closed convex \(C\) is either weakly approachable by player 1 or weakly excludable by player 2, and no counterexample is known that is neither weakly approachable nor weakly excludable in this class.

sources.title: Approachability of convex sets in generalized quitting games
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces three nested geometric conditions (1)–(3) for weak approachability in generalized quitting games; proves Condition (1) is sufficient and Condition (3) is necessary (failure implies weak excludability). Shows weak determinacy holds for Big-Match type I but leaves weak determinacy for Big-Match type II open; provides examples where conditions separate in type II.
sources.connection: This is the source paper formulating the open problem: whether every closed convex target set in Big-Match type II is either weakly approachable by player 1 or weakly excludable by player 2. It provides partial conditions and counterexamples for uniform determinacy, but not for weak determinacy in type II.

sources.title: An analog of the minimax theorem for vector payoffs
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes (uniform) determinacy and a geometric characterization for approachability of closed convex sets in repeated games without absorption: \,\(\max_{y\in\Delta(J)}\min_{x\in\Delta(I)} d_C(g(x,y))=0\) iff \(C\) approachable.
sources.connection: Provides the classical benchmark (Blackwell condition) that fully resolves determinacy in non-absorbing repeated games; the Big-Match type II question asks whether an analogous weak determinacy holds despite absorption controlled by player 2.

sources.title: Weak approachability
sources.theorem_or_result: Proves weak determinacy for general (possibly nonconvex) target sets in classical repeated games by linking weak approachability to the value of an auxiliary differential game.
sources.connection: The source paper adapts Vieille-style differential game ideas to Big-Match type II; a full resolution of weak determinacy in type II is expected to require analogous differential game/value-existence techniques in an absorbing setting.

sources.title: Explicit formulas for repeated games with absorbing states
sources.bibtex:
@article{Laraki2010Explicit,
  title   = {Explicit formulas for repeated games with absorbing states},
  author  = {Laraki, Rida},
  journal = {International Journal of Game Theory},
  volume  = {39},
  number  = {1},
  pages   = {53--69},

}
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives explicit formulas for the asymptotic value in absorbing games using perturbations by measures over actions, motivating expressions of the form \(\frac{g+g^\star}{1+p^\star}\).
sources.connection: Conditions (1)–(3) in the source paper are inspired by Laraki’s measure-perturbation formula for absorbing games; these expressions underpin the candidate geometric criteria for weak approachability/excludability in Big-Match type II.

sources.theorem_or_result: Studies the Big-Match absorbing game; establishes value/uniform guarantees in scalar-payoff settings and foundational techniques for absorbing games.
sources.connection: Big-Match type II is a central absorbing-game model; although scalar-value theory is well developed, the vector-payoff weak determinacy for convex sets remains unresolved.
